text: Non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) serves as a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, facilitating the detection and annotation of diverse small molecules within complex environmental matrices. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) remains the predominant strategy for gathering product ion spectra; however, its effectiveness depends heavily on the optimization of instrument parameters to ensure high spectral quality and broad chemical coverage. In this study, we systematically evaluated the impact of ten key DDA settings on the Q Exactive mass spectrometer using natural organic matter samples sourced from marine, freshwater, and terrestrial environments. Utilizing both classical and feature-based molecular networking via MZmine and GNPS, we assessed performance based on network node density, multivariate clustering, and quality metrics such as annotation rates, MS/MS triggering efficiency, and precursor coverage. Our findings identify *automatic gain control*, *microscans*, *mass resolving power*, and *dynamic exclusion* as the most influential parameters. In contrast, *collision energy*, *TopN*, and *isolation width* exhibited moderate effects, while *apex trigger*, *monoisotopic selection*, and *isotopic exclusion* had minimal impact on the results. These insights into the acquisition dynamics of the Q Exactive platform offer a practical framework for researchers to refine their methods, providing a baseline that may be adaptable to other sample types and mass spectrometry architectures.
